title: Muscle Wasting in Cat's Hind Quarters: Causes & Triage Guide
meta_desc: Noticeable thinning in your cat's back legs? Learn about muscle atrophy, when to see a vet, and how photos help with a diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Hind quarter muscle wasting is typically a chronic sign of underlying issues like arthritis or kidney disease; schedule a vet exam within a week, but seek emergency care if your cat suddenly cannot walk.

What are the symptoms and causes of hind quarter muscle wasting in cats?

Muscle wasting, clinically known as atrophy, is the progressive loss of muscle mass around a cat's hips, thighs, and lower back. When this occurs, you may notice your cat's spine or hip bones becoming more prominent, or their back legs appearing thinner than usual. Common causes include age-related decline (sarcopenia), osteoarthritis, chronic kidney disease, diabetes, or hyperthyroidism.

How urgent is hind quarter muscle wasting and when should I see a vet?

  • Urgency Level: Low. While muscle wasting indicates an underlying health condition, it is typically a chronic issue rather than an acute emergency.
  • Next Steps: Schedule a wellness exam with your veterinarian within the next week.
  • Emergency Warning: Seek immediate veterinary care if the wasting is accompanied by a sudden inability to use the legs, dragging of the limbs, or vocalizing in pain.

How does a photo or video help my vet triage my cat's muscle loss?

  • Visuals allow the vet to assess the symmetry of the muscle loss and observe your cat's gait.
  • Capturing your cat in their natural environment ensures they aren't hiding symptoms due to the stress of a clinic visit.
  • These records serve as a vital baseline to monitor whether the condition is improving or worsening after treatment begins.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Feline hypokalemic polymyopathy, a generalized metabolic muscle weakness disorder, can present with hindlimb weakness secondary to hypokalemia from excessive urinary depletion or inadequate dietary intake. Arterial thromboembolism should also be considered, where the gastrocnemius muscles are often firm, and the cat can move the legs above the stifles; diagnosis is based on clinical signs, physical examination, and Doppler blood flow readings of the hindlimbs. Doppler ultrasonography helps evaluate blood flow in the distal aorta and femoral arteries. Pathologic changes are present distal to the level of the middle to lower thigh and are characterized as focal muscle necrosis and degeneration of the central portions of the sciatic nerve and its branches.

Chapter: Neurology, Musculoskeletal, Metabolic/Nutritional

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1201)

faqs.question: Is muscle wasting just a normal part of aging?
faqs.answer: While some muscle loss occurs naturally with age, significant wasting often points to manageable conditions like arthritis or metabolic disease that can be treated to improve quality of life.

faqs.question: Can diet help restore my cat's muscle mass?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the wasting is caused by nutritional deficiencies or metabolic issues, your vet may prescribe a high-protein or specialized diet to help rebuild or maintain muscle.

faqs.question: What are other signs I should look for?
faqs.answer: Keep an eye out for changes in appetite, increased thirst, difficulty jumping onto surfaces, or changes in litter box habits, as these can help your vet narrow down the cause.
